The law is strict. Section 377 of the Companies Act states that an offence is committed if anyone makes a false or misleading statement knowing it to be false or misleading. The offence is a serious one in our law. It attracts a fine of up to $200000 or a prison term of up to 5 years. If Mr Key were ever convicted of such an offence he would be asked—required—to leave this House.So serious questions arise: did he live in Remuera in March 2002 and did he live in Remuera in April 2003? If he did not in both cases or in either case has he approached the Companies Office to offer an explanation? Does the Companies Office prosecute someone who has a false address or does it prosecute only sometimes and if so under what circumstances? Let me remind the House that my colleague the Hon David Parker when confronted with a similar issue under exactly the same Act stood down. He resumed his ministerial role only when he was able to show that the error he was accused of did not happen. There are some questions to answer I say to Mr Key.Hon BILL ENGLISH (Deputy Leader—National): So in our changing world with the globalised economy the highest interest rates in the developed world and financial instability from here to London here is the big question: did John Key live in Remuera in March 2002? That is it! That is it! Did John Key live in Remuera in March 2002?Hon Trevor Mallard: Is he telling the truth?Hon BILL ENGLISH: I want to tell Mr Mallard what the good voters of Wainuiōmata have as an answer. It is: “Who cares?”. Their other question is: “Trevor why do you care when you always said you cared about ‘me’?”. And that is the problem for the Labour Government. That speech tells everyone who voted Labour that Labour has stopped caring about the voters and cares only and obsessively now about the survival of the Labour Government.The reason we had Mr Hodgson today is that Phil Goff’s performance in the last 10 days has resembled a dog that goes off to chase the traffic and hits a parked car. So today we had the next best shot from the Labour Party Darren Hughes. He was the man who got up in question time and made the big hit. When Labour voters realise that the person Helen Clark is putting into the front line on behalf of low and middle income New Zealanders—people who face 18 months of going backwards between now and the next election—is Darren Hughes then those voters too will start leaving the Labour Party.You see my colleague and our leader John Key is absolutely right. When everyone else thought that the Labour Government was trawling through the policy options to improve the lot of New Zealanders who are now going to pay the price of mismanaged growth it was in fact trawling through the rubbish bins and the Companies Register to try to find another way to trip up a politician whom they simply cannot deal with. That is why it is getting personal and nasty and Trevor Mallard needs to understand thatwhenever he or his colleagues are on TV attacking John Key our support goes up not down.The voters want to see a positive forward-looking vision of New Zealand what potential it has and where it can go rather than see a tired fag-end worn-out bunch of Labour elitists who are only ever worried about where they have been and what jobs they might get between now and the election. That is all summed up by the Electoral Finance Bill. Helen Clark has done nothing more self-serving—more for the Labour Party and against the people—than to bring in that legislation. She has written a piece of legislation that makes sure that her useless Minister—what is his name?Hon David Carter: Burton.Hon BILL ENGLISH: —that he and Trevor Mallard will get to spend $65000 of public money in their electorates next year advertising themselves; and the National candidate will be allowed to spend only $20000. In fact those members will get to spend $85000 $65000 of which is public money. In the electorate of Taupō the National candidate will win.The Government has become totally self-serving and totally inward-looking. I feel sorry for the voters it has deserted because the people who have loyally voted for Labour and expected some kind of reward for it after 8 years have been deserted. Back in 2000 people on the average wage—loyal Labour voters for three elections—were paying 19 percent of their income in tax; now they are paying 23 percent of their income in tax. Their interest rates are going up and their purchasing power is dropping. And if Mr Mallard is worried about the polling now well the bite has not even come yet. [Interruption]Hon CLAYTON COSGROVE (Minister for Building and Construction): Mr Hughes made a good point. I listened to Mr English very carefully. Amongst the things he talked about he alleged that we were a self-serving Government and that it is “all about us”—apparently. Then he spent 2½ minutes talking about what the National Party may get to spend and what the Labour Party may get to spend. He talked about National wanting to get into power and about not liking the fact that we are in power. Well if that is not self-serving I do not know what is.Mr English made another interesting point. He tried to illustrate what the voters want to see. Here is what I think the voters want to see. If John Key wants to become the Prime Minister then the voters will want to see truth consistency and credibility. It does not matter how many speeches that member makes over there whilst every 5 seconds looking up at the press gallery grinning and trying to drag out a bit of adoration because he knows that theNew Zealand Herald for instance on the topic of therapeutic goods sliced him open. He had four dictaphones under his nose and he still could not come out and give a consistent position on that. The New Zealand people want a bit of credible consistent truthful leadership and they ain’t getting it out of that man.We should take a look at Mr Key’s past. Of course it is all right for Bill English and the mob over there to have a go at us on election spending and on all sorts of other issues but when we have a go at them and challenge their leader to be consistent on Iraq on therapeutic goods on where he lives and on climate change which are all pretty simple questions they cannot hack it. They send in the man who is deputy leader who is himself inconsistent. For 8 years on superannuation for instance he said he was not going to cut it then a couple of months ago he said it was too generous and told the old folk that the votes of the 20-year-olds were not won by telling them they would be secure in their retirement and that by the way National might have to cut it and to means test it. So as I said in another speech he is about as consistent about as solid as a jelly on a hotplate; it sort of oozes out from all sides. I say to those members that ifthey want to show real leadership then they should stand up today—not even bothering to give us some policies; we know that is too difficult for them—and tell us what their position is on Iraq on climate change on measures relating to therapeutic goods on where John Key lives and on the other issues Mr Hodgson raised.Those members talk about accountability and they throw arrows across here demanding accountability but when we say: “Hang on a minute you’re inconsistent. The member’s not credible.”—I cannot use the “t” word but their leader is fast and loose with a number of points in his history—they do not like it do they. They cannot hack the pace. But if our democracy is so precious—to quote another member—then the people are owed explanations by all members of this House by all political parties whether they be Government or Opposition parties. We do not ask today for the impossible. We do not ask today for a policy on climate change a policy on housing affordability a policy on therapeutic goods or any of that. That height-bar is just too high for that new member and his deputy. We simply ask on behalf of the New Zealand people that he comes clean on a series of issues.Mr English challenged us to say what we have done for our core constituency. Well I say to Mr English that there have been 350000 new jobs since 1999 and we have the lowest Māori unemployment figures on the record of counting. Under the Working for Families programme 350000 families are getting their own money back in tax credits of on average 110 bucks a week. That can be compared with yet another inconsistent point which is that he wants to gut Working for Families and pull out 50 or 80 bucks a week from that average sum. Opposition members have all gone a bit quiet—the old bantam hens over there—have they not. To quote fromDad’s Army they do not like the old cold steel. Well I say that if they are going to get in the ring throw it about and exercise their democratic right to call individuals in this House to account then they should look in the mirror and be prepared to stand up—especially the Cheshire cat in the back row—and answer some basic questions.Hon MURRAY McCULLY (National—East Coast Bays): These are indeed troubled times for many New Zealanders because they look at the state of the financial markets at the difficulties confronting many as they try to tackle the maintenance of their homeownership and at the fact that something like 750 New Zealanders a week are leaving to live in Australia according to statistics released for the year to the end of July. They look to the people who hold ministerial warrants the people with flash houses and the people with chauffeur-driven limousines and they ask what the Ministers of the Crown in this Government are doing to make their lives more secure.What those Ministers are doing is trawling through the wastepaper baskets behind John Key. That is what the Ministers of the Crown in this Government are doing. That is all they are doing and New Zealanders are entitled to draw the most uncharitable conclusions about that.I have a simple message for the Labour Government today which is that it should stop playing petty politics over New Zealand’s international relationships. In the last week we have seen that playing petty politics with New Zealand’s international relationships comes at a price. It comes at a price for New Zealanders and it has come in particular at a price for New Zealand’s national airline. It is indeed ironic that we should see such outbursts of partisanship at a time when we have the most bipartisan Opposition leader on international relations to sit in this Parliament in over 30 years—the most bipartisan Leader of the Opposition on international relations this Parliament has seen in over 30 years.I would have thought that the Labour Government would embrace such a moment. I would have thought that Labour members would say it is great that they can take international relations off the domestic political agenda. But in the last week and inprevious months we have seen that it does not really matter what it costs New Zealand as long as Labour members can find some cheap line to throw at the political juggernaut that is bowling over the lot of them. I want to remind the House of the cost the national airline will pay and I would ask the Ministers opposite whether they will give us information on this because New Zealanders deserve to know.Mr Goff and other Ministers decided to drag Mr Key’s name through debates in this Chamber by trying to bring international relations unnecessarily into the political debate. They were prepared to put almost anything at risk. We have seen as a consequence that the Minister’s relationship with the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade has been put at risk that the relationship with the national flag carrier has been put at risk and most important that our most important international relationship—that with Australia—has been put at risk. At the end of last week we would have thought that they had learnt their lesson that they would draw a line under this and move on but just yesterday we saw the Minister of Defence—the former Minister of Foreign Affairs and Trade—yet again taking another cheap shot in the direction of the Australian Minister for Foreign Affairs. I say that it is time that that tactic stopped.I want the Ministers opposite to tell this House today just what the cost will be for Air New Zealand. We know that $18 million worth of charters are now at risk but what New Zealanders have yet to grasp is that Air New Zealand conducts a great deal of work for the Australian Defence Force. It does a great deal of mechanical engineering work for the Royal Australian Air Force and I want Ministers to tell us just how many of those contracts have been put at risk. What will the Australian Government’s position mean for those contracts? What will it mean for new contracts? New contracts are coming up for tender in the coming weeks. Will New Zealand be able to tender for those contracts?We know that the Royal Australian Navy uses Air New Zealand’s workshops for significant work on the gas turbines on their frigates. Are those contracts at risk?Darren Hughes: John Key agrees with Helen Clark on this.Hon MURRAY McCULLY: Well I think that New Zealanders are entitled to know what the cost is. We know that Air New Zealand derives in total some tens of millions of dollars of revenue from those contracts and we want an answer.Hon NANAIA MAHUTA (Minister of Customs): New Zealanders are entitled to know National’s policy on anything that its members have raised in this House today. It is a very interesting debate when we hear members of the Opposition getting up and saying a whole lot of things but not being prepared to put some substance behind those things. It is a typical Opposition strategy to create a doom and gloom image say anything bring in the marketing guru and then say anything again. That has been the strategy adopted by National members.To put it quite simply National is not reflecting the real concerns of people on the ground. In fact what we are hearing out there is something to this effect: “You know John Key says all the right things but we’re not sure about the detail. You get the feeling he’s going to say what he thinks you want to hear but not what he really believes in. When are we really going to hear the detail? We know the policy but what are they really going to do? We have had enough of the marketing strategy.” That is what New Zealanders out on the ground are saying but National wants us to believe otherwise.Labour has been listening to the real issues out there that people care about the real issues that they believe this Government is addressing the real issues that they know make a difference to the lives of families—families with children the elderly and people who are disadvantaged within our communities. When we get that feeling that there is a bit of a movement and a swelling on the ground that John Key says all theright things but that people are not sure about the detail—they get the feeling he will say what he thinks they want to hear but not what he believes—we know that there is a marketing strategy there that is just for TV and just for Parliament that has nothing to do with people out there on the ground in the community. I can tell by the way National members are looking down at the floor that they know it too.Dealing with real issues is about affecting the lives of people in our communities for example through establishing low-cost visits to general practitioners and low-cost prescriptions. Those affect real people real lives. Income-related rentals affect real people real lives so does ensuring that in our communities we have more skilled and trained people. Just last night I was in the south Waikato district at a ceremony to celebrate the increased number of Modern Apprentices who are now coming through in Tokoroa. It was fantastic. The mayor himself said that never before had they had such commitment from the Government from industry training organisations and from local businesses to say that the trade sector was a priority. Small communities like Tokoroa are growing their own apprentices and now those apprentices have the ability to contribute back to their communities.That is what real people are feeling on the ground. They are quite excited actually about the level of investment that this Government is making alongside communities to improve the lives of a lot of people. More important my colleague Clayton Cosgrove pointed to the issue of employment and to the increased number of jobs we have had out there. We know that affecting the real lives of people and families with children is done through giving people the ability to have a job put kai on the table and look after their own. That is what people want and that is what they have seen under this Government. There are also rates rebates for low-income homeowners. Again that is a policy that affects the real lives of real people.But what would National have us believe? They would have us believe that all that does not matter and that it is not making a difference. We can go into any small community and its people will tell us that Government policies have gone quite a bit towards helping them meet their annual costs.What is most important is that we are investing in families with children through tax credits not through tax cuts that would benefit only a few. Investing in families with children through the Working for Families package has continued to make a difference. We can always do more there and we intend to. National is very light on detail but what it is not telling people is that that investment would go under a National Government.So although the marketing strategy may work for Parliament and for TV those people out on the ground real families with real commitments who have a real sense of wanting to do better for themselves know that if National gets in all that will go. Out on the ground people know that although National members are so hungry to get on to the Treasury benches that they will say what people want right up until the election after the election bang they will do something else.That is at the core of the question asked by my colleague Pete Hodgson. We have to be able to believe what someone says and we want to have a sense that at the very top of the Opposition leadership the Leader of the Opposition is able to tell the truth and to say what he believes not what he thinks people want to hear. That is the kind of difference that I think in time will reveal itself and will really show a lot of New Zealanders that that leadership is all style no substance and no detail whatsoever. New Zealanders deserve better than that.BARBARA STEWART (NZ First): Kiwi cancer patients are suffering needlessly because the technology that could streamline their treatment is not available publicly here in New Zealand and it is not likely to be for quite some time. It is not fair it is notjust and it is blatantly wrong. The Ministry of Health has been dithering around about the purchase of a publicly funded PET scanner which is a positron emission tomography scanner since mid-2005 and we are still no closer. A recent TV3 news report stated that we are about 3 years away from that—3 long years. It is a totally unbelievable wait for cancer sufferers and for cancer doctors who are also pushing for one of these publicly funded PET scanners.It is a fact that these scanners have been available in Australia since 1992. The patient is injected with a very small amount of radioactive glucose which provides a very clear image of cancer sites and gives doctors a very clear indication of the stage of the tumour and what treatment is required and of course it is used for monitoring after the therapy to see whether the tumour is still alive. PET scanning has become a benchmark for staging cancer and New Zealanders have great difficulty accessing it. An 84 percent accuracy in the diagnosis of cancer is as important as an 86 percent specificity of the rate of cancer. Basically a PET scanner ensures the best treatment option for that particular patient.Over in Australia—and I am definitely not one to push the boat of Australia—they work on a ratio of having one to 1.5 publicly funded PET scanners for every 1 million of their population. They currently have 16 scanners in 13 locations seven contracted facilities and one cyclotron to produce the radioactive material for every four scanners. So the Australians have at least four cyclotrons and they are actually looking at increasing their PET facilities.In New Zealand we do not have a publicly funded scanner. We would love to have one. We would like to have one cyclotron as well. It would be very beneficial. Two would be beyond belief. What do we have here in New Zealand? We have patients travelling to Australia at their own expense. Last year 43 patients paid their own way to Australia for a scan and 53 patients were funded by district health boards. I would imagine that not one of those patients came from the far north or the East Coast. There is a facility at Wakefield Hospital and some private patients and some very fortunate people from Capital and Coast actually get to use it. The glucose solution is flown in from Australia pretty regularly.We need our own facility in New Zealand. We know that it could cost up to $20 million to set one up but one surgeon estimated that the savings on lung cancer alone could be up to $600000 per year. Overall it has been estimated that at least $3.5 million is wasted on unnecessary surgery for cancer patients. We know that cancer causes 29 percent of all deaths in this country and it is estimated that by 2011 this will increase to 47 percent which is estimated to be 22000 cancer patients.Research that could save lives and expedite treatment should be a priority for the Ministry of Health. But what has happened? The bureaucracy is spinning into action and committees have been formed—a real committee-fest. The Cancer Treatment Working Party presented a report to the Ministry of Health in mid-2005. The National Service and Technology Review Advisory Committee provided an update to the Minister of Health in November 2006. We have a Service Planning and New Health Intervention Assessment committee looking at new health interventions. That committee is somehow involved. Then the business case was considered by the national District Health Boards chief executive group in June 2007 and it was turned down. The chief executive officer group decided that it was too costly without adequate justification of additional patient benefit which is ridiculous.Dr WAYNE MAPP (National—North Shore): Dysfunction disorganisation—these are the hallmarks of a disintegrating Government. I have to ask the Government speakers: who knew what happened in the Air New Zealand situation? The strangest aspect of the whole issue about Air New Zealand and charter flights is who in theGovernment actually knew the truth. According to the Prime Minister the Minister of Defence and the Minister of Foreign Affairs they knew only last week when the story broke. But in fact most of the senior Government officials—the senior public servants—knew and had known for months. Simon Murdoch had been specifically advised by Rob Fyfe the Chief Executive Officer of Air New Zealand that the flights were to take place. Air New Zealand did the right thing. It knew that the flights were sensitive so it sought advice and spoke to the most senior foreign affairs official in the country—actually chief executive officer to chief executive officer.The information did not remain just with Mr Murdoch. There is a special committee—the Officials Committee for Domestic and External Security Coordination—of which Mr Murdoch is a member. The committee is chaired by Mr Maartin Wevers who is the head of the Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet and it includes the Secretary of Defence Mr John McKinnon. These three people are all former or current diplomats of the highest experience. One would think they knew how to spot a sensitive issue. Other members on the committee include the head of defence the head of SIS the police commissioner and the chief executive of Treasury. They all knew yet apparently no one spoke to a Minister or even to anyone in a Minister’s office.Now we have to take a member’s word in this House—or as the Prime Minister says until it is proven otherwise. That is the formula she used to get rid of Mr Benson-Pope. So we have to ask this simple question: why was it that these experienced public servants thought that they did not have to speak to a single Minister even though Air New Zealand could see there was a potential problem? It is the case that this Government seems to have two foreign policies—one for external consumption and one for local consumption. We saw the local consumption version last week. The Government always attacks the United States—the bogeyman of the left. It happened at the last election. We saw Phil Goff give one of his vein-popping speeches. The Government thinks there is mileage in all of that but Colin James said this week in theNew Zealand Herald that the Government should forget Iraq; the country is simply not interested.The Government also has a different foreign policy—the external one. It is usually a bit of a different message: we like to get on with friendly Governments smoothing over differences and putting them off to the margins—although that was somewhat forgotten by the Prime Minister in reference to Australia this week. One has to ask oneself this question: is this why the officials thought that the flights were OK? Had the Government somehow given an indication that they should be helpful to our friends that all the Government Ministers needed was some form of plausible deniability? On the face of it that is exactly what they had. They came down here and denied.I reckon the public have worked this one out. They can see hypocrisy. They can sense shallow politics. This time the tired old tactic of bashing the United States of bashing our closest friend Australia simply has not worked. I wonder whether any of the members on the other side of the House have actually taken the time and trouble to look at theNew Zealand Herald blogsite. There are about 30 pages on this issue and almost 95 percent are saying that they actually do not care that it does not really matter that it is probably a good thing that we help out our old mate Australia when it asks for it and that Australia is entitled to a bit of respect and support. The public simply do not care.Russell Fairbrother: Off over to Iraq are you?Dr WAYNE MAPP: That is the point that Mr Fairbrother does not work out. The public have moved on. I must make this point to Mr Fairbrother. Mr James was absolutely right. I say to the Government to forget about Iraq because the public areactually looking forward. They are not interested in disputes of the past. They want change and come November 2008 they will have their chance to get it.Hon MARK GOSCHE (Labour—Maungakiekie): That member Wayne Mapp wants everybody to forget Iraq. So do all the Governments that have sent troops there who have died. The National Party wants to sweep it under the carpet. We can understand that; its members do not want the words of their leader coming back to haunt them. They would prefer it to be forgotten it is sad that it will not be forgotten and it should not be forgotten by New Zealanders. If that lot were the Government they would have sent young New Zealand men and women there to die and Air New Zealand would have been bringing them back in body bags.Dr Wayne Mapp: Oh no.Hon MARK GOSCHE: Yes that is what it would have been doing. Poor old Dr Mapp wants the public to forget it.But let us get back to some of the earlier comments and speeches about the poor personal attacks on Mr Key and about how sad it is that the Government wants to remind the public about his shallowness. I was at a meeting a couple of weeks ago of about 400 factory workers in South Auckland. National says we are not in touch and that we do not listen to those people. Well I was there and we were talking about housing. Of course we have had the National members lately pronouncing on housing. What have they been saying? They have said that they want to sell State houses to the tenants yet all of this year National’s housing spokesperson Mr Heatley has been railing about the properties in Ōrākei in the eastern suburbs of Auckland. It is a place where many people particularly Ngāti Whātua have lived for years and years despite Governments doing their very best to shove them out and he is saying that we should sell those properties. Will he sell them to the tenants? Will they have the $1 million to buy them?Jill Pettis: No!Hon MARK GOSCHE: No! National will be selling them to its mates yet again. Let us get back to Mr Key’s words. He is afraid of the effects on the underclass. He believes that it is unaffordable for many people to have a house. So what is this Government doing about it? We will actually do something about it up in Hobsonville. We will have affordable housing for people to buy. We will have State housing we will have schools and we will have shops. We will make a community where not everybody in that community is rich or poor because we know the failed policies of the past and what they delivered.What does the man who went up to Waitangi with a young Māori girl saying that he was there for the underclass say about Hobsonville State housing? What does he say about this Government’s desire to have affordable housing in his electorate? He says: “Oh no! We don’t want those poor people anywhere near us. Get them out of here. And by the way do not let them live in Ōrākei either.” This is the man who would have us believe that he is there for the underclass for all of those people I was talking to at that meeting in South Auckland who work in the factories who work in manufacturing jobs and who are trying to bring up families on low or modest incomes. What does Mr Key have in store for them? He says that we cannot have affordable housing anywhere near his electorate that we cannot have low-income people living in $1 million properties up in Ōrākei—they have to be sold—and he pretends that he will sell State houses to low-income people who are in State houses right now because they are so low-income that they cannot afford to buy a house. Where is the logic in that?But we should not get personal. We should not get in there and test his theories and policies. Oh no it is terrible if the Government dares to do that to the Leader of the Opposition. The same people Mr Key says he is concerned about—the people I wastalking to a couple of weeks ago—were saying how great KiwiSaver is. They asked me what the National Party would do to it. We know what it will do to it because it has already said that this Government is spending too much on retirement. What that means is that National will either cut KiwiSaver or cut the pension or both. Those members over there know that. They know that the party led by John Key voted against increasing the minimum wage voted against KiwiSaver voted against increased holidays for average working families and voted against lower doctor fees. It voted against prescription fees of three bucks. This is the man who says that he is worried about the underclass yet he voted against lowering doctor fees lowering prescription costs better wages and better holidays.Of course we are not supposed to remind him about his desire to send troops to Iraq so that they could be killed. It would not be a bunch of Tories’ sons and daughters who went over there and fought in Iraq; that is not the way it works anywhere in the world. Oh no the poor underclass would provide the bodies to go over there. [Interruption] Dr Mapp does not want to be reminded about Iraq; he wants to read hisNew ZealandHerald blog so that he can forget his leader’s idiotic statements which he made 3 or 4 years ago.CHRISTOPHER FINLAYSON (National): There has been much criticism of the Minister of Justice Mark Burton in recent weeks and all of it is deserved. He is simply not up to the job. His performance in question time when trying to answer Bill English’s incisive questions has been nothing short of incompetent.Hon Trevor Mallard: You can tell which side this one’s on!CHRISTOPHER FINLAYSON: But for the benefit of Mr Mallard I say that I am in a charitable mood this afternoon. To borrow the words of Denis Healey—who was a real Labour person as opposed to Mr Mallard—I want to concentrate on the realMephistopheles behind this shabby and discredited Faust. I want to talk about the Attorney-General.There is one matter in the debate on the Electoral Finance Bill that has to be addressed and that is the failure of the Attorney-General to provide this House with a report under section 7 of the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act.Jill Pettis: That’s in touch with the people!CHRISTOPHER FINLAYSON: The former member for Whanganui is hiding behind two boxes. I wonder whether it is in order for her to do that and to yell and to scream like a fishwife. She should come out from behind those boxes if she wants to interject properly.The Crown Law Office provided a legal opinion to assist the Attorney-General to determine whether a report should be made to Parliament. The Crown Law Office concluded by a fine margin that the bill is a justifiable limitation on the right of freedom of expression. This was a very poor opinion; it failed to address fundamental issues about this legislation—that it impairs the ability of private citizens to criticise public institutions and political candidates and that it stops private citizens from debating matters of public interest during an election year.Those are the sorts of restrictions that are worthy of Boy Assad of Syria; they are Draconian and they are totally unacceptable. The Attorney-General should have provided a report under section 7. In fact I agree with former Labour MP Richard Northey who said in this House in August 1990 that the Attorney-General has an individual independent responsibility to act in a judicial way and to report on matters even when there may be political pressures not to notice them.Any Attorney-General conscious of his or her responsibilities would have provided a section 7 report in this case notwithstanding the Crown Law Office opinion. I agree with one of the Attorney-General’s predecessors the Hon Margaret Wilson when shesaid that as Attorney-General she considered she should err on the side of caution in advising the House of any possible inconsistency with the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act. It is for that reason that she provided a section 7 report on the Electoral (Public Opinion Polls) Amendment Bill 2000 and the Electoral Amendment Bill (No 2) 2001. In both those cases she concluded that the bills were inconsistent with section 14 of the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act and did not appear to be justified under section 5. In both those cases the House was dealing with members’ bills but that is beside the point. She was conscious of her responsibilities and took the time and trouble to write such a report.When the New Zealand Bill of Rights legislation was being debated in 1990 some National MPs criticised the reporting duty of the Attorney-General and said it was ridiculous to expect the Attorney-General who is bound by the principle of collective responsibility to write a report contrary to Government policy. Labour MPs however emphasised the independence of the Attorney-General and said that he or she could.This Attorney-General is a disgrace. He has put party before Parliament and the Labour Party’s interests before the public interest. The Attorney-General is the person who should show his strength by critically analysing this rotten legislation in New Zealand Bill of Rights Act terms but he has instead decided to remain silent. Being the Attorney-General is about more than appointing the occasional judge and overseeing the work of the Crown Law Office. The Attorney-General must be an independent officer placing the interests of the law and the fundamental rights of New Zealanders above party. This Attorney-General has failed to do so and stands condemned for his failure.It is very interesting that the Labour Party benches have fallen silent on this issue because it is a major constitutional issue. I would be very interested to hear from the one lawyer in the Labour Party caucus for whom I have respect namely Mr Fairbrother. What does Mr Fairbrother say about this issue? Why did the Attorney-General not provide a report at the time of introduction of this legislation? This was as the Crown Law Office said even putting it most generously a finely balanced matter. It deserved a report. The Attorney-General failed in his responsibilities in failing to provide one. This Attorney-General is a Labour Party hack and as I said he stands condemned because he has put his loyalty to the Labour Party to the tribe above his public responsibilities.RUSSELL FAIRBROTHER (Labour):That member Christopher Finlayson is the man who styles himself as the Attorney-General in waiting—waiting being the operative word. That man will be waiting when Charles Chauvel becomes the Attorney-General. That man will be waiting when Charles Chauvel turns 50. That man will never make Attorney-General because he will never be on the Treasury benches. That man will leave this House as frustrated and bitter as he entered it. But what that man does represent is the moneyed right. He is the man who aspires to be Attorney-General who featured in the bookThe Hollow Men and who held high-priced fund-raising dinners so that the wealthy right wing in this country could buy their way into power. He is the man who criticises our Attorney-General claiming a lack of independence. The facts speak for themselves.Let us have a look at the credibility issue. This is not an attack on John Key. He does not need an attack from me; he attacks himself every time he opens his mouth. This is an attack on his credibility.This is a debate. The debate was set off by a lacklustre speech made by the Leader of the Opposition John Key. The only thing he told the House was that he is presently ahead in the polls. He dwelled upon that as though it were a matter of great moment of State.The reply to that was led by our Minister of Health Pete Hodgson. He brought facts and figures into the debate. He outlined the inconsistency of statutory declarations bythe Leader of the Opposition. That man who wants to be Attorney-General knows how important statutory declarations are. If we trifle with them we are committing a serious moral offence at least and perhaps a serious legal offence. Pete Hodgson outlined chapter and verse the impossibility for all those statutory declarations to be honest to be straightforward and to be taken at face value. And we have a motive for them to be misleading. At the time those declarations were made John Key was standing for the first time in the seat of Helensville. He was styling himself as a man of the people of that electorate. Of course it is quite clear now that he had no intention of delivering on his promise that he would live in the area. The statutory declarations outlined by Pete Hodgson today show quite clearly the total lack of credibility that man has.But John Key has a remedy. At any time during the speech made by the Minister of Health or after—or even now—he could stand in this House and invoke Standing Order 133(g) to interrupt the debate and make a personal statement to clarify the position. His credibility having been put on the line in this House this afternoon in this debate has not been restored by him. Why? Because he cannot advance a statement that will repair the damage of those inconsistent statutory declarations. Therefore as we stand in the House right now the debating speech of Pete Hodgson has to be taken as accurate and accepted by the Leader of the Opposition. It has to tell us that he filed conflicting statutory declarations when he should have been consistent forthright and honest.What does that tell us about the man who comes here and gives a lacklustre speech gloating that he is ahead in the polls at this moment? It tells us that he is a very good marketer. What is a marketer? A marketer is someone who creates a need in someone’s mind when that person does not want the product for which the need is created. What is the product they are creating? They are creating a myth of beneficence. I sat on a select committee this morning with some of the members sitting on the other side of the House and those members must really have been churning in their stomachs at the evidence we heard.My friend Mr Gosche spoke about an underclass. The underclass in this country are not the people whom John Key wants to highlight. The underclass in this country is truly a category of people who are marginalised by a system that fails to recognise their personal needs and their view of the world. I hate to use that term “underclass” but it is the only term by which we can describe people who are marginalised and discriminated against in such a way.This is the world of the disabled. [Interruption] The member can interrupt about the disabled; he certainly can. He wants to forget them just as he wants to forget about his party’s promise to go to Iraq. He wants to forget about the disabled and he certainly will if he ever gets to the Treasury benches. The world of the disabled is a very very marginalised and sad world. Their complaint of course is that the money available to the disability sector is not getting to them. But they should wait until we have tax cuts and wait until we have members on the other side of the Chamber on the Treasury benches. Then there will be no money for the marginalised and discriminated against disabled sector.The debate having concluded the motion lapsed.Points of OrderSignage in the Chamber—New Zealand Labour PartyNATHAN GUY (Junior Whip—National): I raise a point of order Madam Speaker. It is my understanding that at the Business Committee this week it was ruled that the red boxes that Labour Party members have on their benches need to be with the member or taken with the member when that member moves. I look across the Housenow and I raise the point with you Madam Speaker that there are three of these silly red boxes on the other side of the Chamber but only one member is sitting there.Hon TREVOR MALLARD (Minister for Economic Development): I say to you Madam Temporary Speaker that Madam Speaker did rule on this today. She indicated that members would be informed of the Business Committee’s ruling in the appropriate way and after that it would be enforced. If the member had been listening at the time and if he had been awake at the time—I know it is unusual in the afternoon for the member—he would have heard that.The TEMPORARY SPEAKER (Jill Pettis): When Madam Speaker was in the Chair during question time she said precisely that. She will be reporting to the Business Committee at the first available opportunity as to the ongoing action regarding the presentation of boxes in the House.Dr WAYNE MAPP (National—North Shore): The only point I would make is that the boxes actually belong to the junior whip and he attends the Business Committee so he can hardly claim ignorance of this matter.The TEMPORARY SPEAKER (Jill Pettis): Communication can be made in a variety of ways around here. Madam Speaker has indicated that she will do that.Major Events Management BillIn CommitteePart 1 Preliminary provisionsALLAN PEACHEY (National—Tamaki): The National Party is of course supporting this legislation. National members have been very happy to work with the Government and with other parties in the Commerce Committee to make sure that the Major Events Management Bill is tidy legislation and legislation that reflects the balance that will be needed to make sure it works.It is quite a significant step for New Zealand to move forward and think about our ability as a country to actually host major events and the 2011 Rugby World Cup is I understand the third-largest sporting event in the world. So it is a great credit to the New Zealand Rugby Union and to those who work with the Rugby Union to secure that. But it means that we have to do it right because if we do not do it right other sporting organisations will not entrust similar responsibilities to New Zealand. That is what this bill is pretty much about. It sets up the right regulatory framework and puts in place the right sort of legislation so that when the opportunity arises we can bid for and secure these events and do them well.There is a high test on the definition of “major event” and that is how it should be. There will be a temptation for various groups and organisations that are hosting events to want to elevate their event to the major event level. This bill—passed in its present form—should prevent that from happening. The test of what is a major event must be very high because balanced against that are some definitions of restrictions on what individual New Zealanders and business interests can do when this Act is applied—so we want to get that right. A lot of the work in the select committee was centred on making sure that the balance was right.When I considered the purpose of the bill I reflected on some of the arguments that have been put up against the restriction on ambush marketing and the suggestions that this sort of legislation is in actual fact anti-entrepreneurial. That is absolute nonsense of course and I ask members who have run that argument and who decide this afternoon to run it again to just reflect on something. That something is that we will not get events of the size of the 2011 Rugby World Cup in New Zealand if we are not prepared to put in place some protections for those who invest in the venture. In my mind it is nodifferent at all from the protections that we give to for example science or medical research companies that have the opportunity having invested sometimes hundreds—Ron Mark: Ha ha!ALLAN PEACHEY: Well the member for New Zealand First may think it is funny but I suggest he reflect on what I am about to say. The incentive for companies to invest heavily in that sort of research lies in the opportunities they have to maximise the returns from the product once the research is completed the product is tested and the product goes on the market. That is how it should be and investment in something like the Rugby World Cup needs to be seen in a similar context. Businesses are not going to make thoughtful carefully thought-out decisions in terms of putting up the millions of dollars that are required to underwrite events like this if they are not going to have a fair opportunity to get a fair return from their investment.So instead of talking about this bill being in some way anti-entrepreneurial members need to see it in the wider context of the motivation for business to invest in a venture. Frankly it does not really matter whether the venture is the 2011 Rugby World Cup or one of the two or three other major events that New Zealand is hoping to host in the next 10 years or so.KEITH LOCKE (Green): It is rather strange to hear the National Party backing up the Government in this Chamber and in this case supporting the force of the State being used to prevent businesses from operating and to prevent competition. That is what this bill is all about. It is about the heavy hand of the State and supporting it is hardly something I would expect from parties that claim they are in favour of the free market free competition and all the rest of it.When one actually looks at the definitions in this part one sees that in clause 3 “Purposes” a purpose is to “obtain maximum benefits from the major event for New Zealanders;”. I do not see how it gets maximum benefit for New Zealanders to say prevent Air New Zealand from advertising 5 kilometres out along a clean transport route as declared under this bill because say Emirates Airline is the official sponsor of the 2011 Rugby World Cup—as Emirates Airline is the sponsor of the Rugby World Cup soon to take place in France. How does it help New Zealand businesses if we stop them from advertising 5 kilometres away from an event venue—be it Air New Zealand against Emirates Airline or Lemon and Paeroa against Coca-Cola which is the sponsor of the Rugby World Cup this year and maybe in 2011? I think that is a bit strange.The clause further states in subclause (2)(ca) that the legislation “prohibits activities that might compromise the smooth running of a major event such as ticket scalping and pitch invasion;”. At first sight I wonder how ticket scalping—whether or not we like it—affects the smooth running of an event. I understood that ticket scalping was the selling of tickets and that is a bit different from what happens on a rugby field or in the stands. Again the anti-competitive streak in the National Party and in Labour seems to be motivating that provision.Of course we do not like people to make huge fortunes out of onselling tickets but do we really want to put in a whole bureaucratic procedure to stop what is effectively a trade in second-hand goods? Any decent organiser of a major event will distribute the tickets quite widely and some people may end up with one or two extra and may make a bit of a profit out of them but is that really such a huge problem in society? With regard to the Rugby Sevens to be held in Wellington next year the tickets go on sale next month. People can get them on the Internet and there will be a lot of competition for tickets. I do not think anyone is going to end up with a huge number of tickets to onsell so how will that affect the running of the event?And how will inventing a whole new offence of pitch invasion help with the smooth running of the event? Disorderly behaviour is already an offence on the law books. Sofar anyone who has disrupted a sporting event by running on the field has been charged with disorderly behaviour; we do not need that new offence in order to allow the smooth running of events. I think that this bill is completely unnecessary.The bill also defines what a major event is. The previous speaker said it would have to meet a high test but as I understand it the event—and there is flexibility about this in terms of the views of the Government of the day—has to have international visitors of some substance and have international advertising associated with it. That would apply to the Bledisloe Cup and to a Super 14 final if it had a foreign team and a lot of Aussies coming across the Tasman say. It would certainly apply to the Rugby Sevens which comes up next year.I turn to the definition in the bill of a clean transport route. Why do we need clean transport routes? That is the heavy hand of the State and I will be moving a Supplementary Order Paper to remove that. I hope to get support for it from other parties. I know several other parties will be supporting the Greens on that and I just hope that either Labour or National will too.MARYAN STREET (Labour): I rise to speak to this Major Events Management Bill in the Committee stage noting that we are talking about the purposes the interpretation the status of examples and the fact that the Act binds the Crown as set out in Part 1 of the bill. I wish to keep my comments to those matters.This bill is quite straightforward in its aims and purposes. It states quite clearly that its purposes are to obtain the maximum benefit for New Zealanders from major events held in New Zealand to prevent unauthorised commercial exploitation of major events through ambush marketing activity and to ensure the smooth running of major events. I think the comments that we heard from the previous speaker Keith Locke absolutely underscore the purpose of this bill. In respect of the example of pitch invasion yes there has been a move from using laws around disorderly behaviour to using a particular offence of pitch invasion.Notwithstanding the comments from Mr Mark in a previous reading of this bill about those of us who may have worn such activities as badges in our past it is important when a country is running and hosting a major event of the nature that is defined in this bill that the purpose of it is maintained and the running of it is orderly. This is in order that the event may proceed first of all without damage to the event itself; secondly without damage by ambush marketing to the integrity of the sponsorship that has allowed the event to be held in the first place; and thirdly without bringing our own reputation into international disrepute at events that are major international fixtures.I think the purposes of this bill are admirable. I am pleased with the way the Commerce Committee members from across the parties have supported the purposes and the interpretations of this bill. There is one further comment I make. I may be wrong in this but it seems to me that inserting examples into legislation is novel. I find it an extremely helpful practice. When we are talking about such things as ambush marketing by association or intrusion it is extremely elucidating to be able to point to an illustration or example in the text of the legislation that makes it patently obvious what is intended by those terms. Although I consider the interpretations to be adequate in themselves the fact that there are examples included within the body of this bill makes it eminently accessible. I think that is a very desirable trait particularly as this Committee moves to try to introduce legislation in plain English. I commend the drafters and the Minister for the introduction of such examples into the text because they make the bill very very accessible.This is legislation that will not only facilitate the running of events but as it says in the purpose clause facilitate the benefits for New Zealanders and in fact allow for some integrity to be maintained around commercial sponsorship and therefore increasethe attractiveness to sponsors of events such as the Rugby World Cup. Thank you Madam Chair.GERRY BROWNLEE (National—Ilam): I am very happy to make a contribution on the purpose part of the Major Events Management Bill. The speaker before me Maryan Street outlined what the purpose is and I do not want to reiterate those words but in essence the purpose is also to advance New Zealand’s wider interests by hosting events in areas where we are particularly prominent. The Rugby World Cup of course is one of those events the Cricket World Cup will follow and I am sure there will be other big international events that will be able to avail themselves of the provisions of this particular law and these particular arrangements for their events to be equally successful.Anyone who looks at the effect that the Rugby World Cup can have on our economy not only in the year or the month that the event is held but in the years leading up to it and the years subsequent to it would agree that it is appropriate that we make arrangements to ensure the event’s success. It is interesting to note that much of New Zealand’s economy in the last couple of years has been somewhat insulated from the decline in the rest of the world’s tourism markets and that this is apparently attributable to the success of theLord of the Ringsfilms. I do not doubt that that is a fact and I congratulate all those people who were involved in those films. But it needs to be recognised that the number of people who will watch any one or all three of the Lord of the Rings trilogy pales into insignificance compared with the number of people who will watch one or more games during the Rugby World Cup.I recall last year going to an office not too far from here where a large number of people had gathered to watch the Tour de France the bike race that happens in France each year.Hon Trevor Mallard: I’m surprised you got up that early Gerry.GERRY BROWNLEE: Actually it was a replay. The Minister has said that he is surprised I got up as early as that. In fact I was up as early as that; I work very long hours. In the small part of the day that I normally reserve for sleep I was watching a replay with a group of people who interestingly were not making comments about the race itself but were constantly commenting about the beautiful background and the scenery through which the race was happening. A similar thing will happen with the Rugby World Cup. More eyes will look at this country more eyes will see what we are about and more people will make a decision to come here and effectively bring their import dollars into this country. Given that we have such a big reliance—around 40 percent of our domestic economy—on tourism it is an appropriate focus.I want to pick up on a couple of remarks made by Keith Locke. The first thing is that things like the Rugby Sevens will not be affected by this bill at all. The Rugby Sevens event is not a major event and is unlikely to be declared so. It is a very major event for this part of the world but it is not a nationwide event and therefore would not be caught by this legislation. Secondly I want to send a bit of a message in that regard to the organisers of the Rugby World Cup. It is pretty depressing for me standing here today supporting this bill and wanting it to be successful to have read in my local newspaper that my city of Christchurch is unlikely to get to host a semi-final of the Rugby World Cup. I think that this is serious because the promotion around this thing has been that 4 million fans—in other words the whole country—are getting in behind this. It is unacceptable to simply say: “Well you know for the sake of 20000 seats we are going to move the semi-finals and the final to Auckland.”Hon Trevor Mallard: It’s logistics.GERRY BROWNLEE: Do not give me the logistics argument. This is a small country. Auckland is an hour away from Christchurch by plane and I am sure it not toohard to organise that. Given that Christchurch is the largest centre in the South Island the reality is that the stadium could easily have been filled by locals anyway. So some of the thinking there needs to change if people expect this event to still be considered New Zealand’s Rugby World Cup rather than Auckland’s Rugby World Cup. So there is a small message there.Around the issue of pitch invasion Mr Locke appeared to make the case that this should be perfectly acceptable. I recall Mr Locke walking down Queen Street in his underwear and I would hate to see him repeat that sort of activity at any one of the Rugby World Cup games because that would be the wrong image for New Zealand. Similarly having the pitch invaded by anyone creates a poor impression of this country. We had a very interesting submission at the Commerce Committee from a young woman called Lisa Lewis. She was a very intelligent young woman and she advanced the view that we should enable some form of pitch invasion. She went on to say that Wikipedia attributes the invention of the art of bikini streaking to her; therefore it is a New Zealand first—no pun on the party of course I say to Mr Mark but a bit of that may help with the votes. She was saying that this sort of thing should be allowed.But as the conversation progressed it became evident that what she was really saying was that at the start of a match the conclusion of a match and half-time there needed to be some form of entertainment and that maybe this form of entertainment could be considered. Under this provision if the event organisers chose to have those sorts of promotions that would be their prerogative provided that whatever they were doing was within the law and was I suppose tastefully presented if I could put it that way. The reality is that all these games these days have pre-match entertainment they have entertainment at half-time and I suspect that they will have something at the conclusion of these games as well. Pitch invasion though is the opportunity for people to impinge on the activity that other people have paid a lot of money to see. No one has a right to do that. We should just simply codify that so the whole world knows where we stand on that particular issue.When it comes to clean transport zones I think there is a whole lot of misunderstanding about what is actually meant. Essentially they are about trying to protect the interests of sponsors who put up a lot of money to make these events fly in the first place. I do not think it is appropriate that people should be able to engage in ambush marketing activities that effectively see them get the credit for the sponsorship. At the select committee we were given a very good example of how this works. The example involved Germany where the football world cup was played. The major sponsor for that event was an airline. It put up a very very significant amount of money to make it work. It tied up its own promotion around the event. But a competing airline carried out the simple exercise of putting on its aeroplanes a decoration that conveyed the clear message that it was the sponsor of that event.At the conclusion of the event a large number of people who had participated as spectators or otherwise were of the view that the competing airline had been the main sponsor. So the poor old airline that had put all the money in and made the event happen was robbed of some of the benefit. I think we have to be realistic about this. It really did fascinate us to see the breadth of creativity that people can engage in in order to ambush the marketing around one of these events.So in response to Mr Locke I say we need to be very relaxed about the provisions in this bill. They are not about destroying business in this country. This entire bill is about giving business in this country an enormous opportunity to grow its activities and to do so for the benefit of the whole country. I am sure that at some stage the Minister will want to share with the Committee the value that New Zealand got out of the recent yachting event at Seville. Was it Seville?Jill Pettis: Valencia.GERRY BROWNLEE: Well I knew it was the name of an orange so I was halfway there. The America’s Cup was very successful for New Zealand businesses. They were able to spin off the back of that. We should not be frightened about the Government actually backing New Zealand. In many ways anyone who argues against this bill argues against that premise.This is a good bill. I think the Committee will make very few changes to it because of the way in which it was dealt with by the Commerce Committee. The committee dealt with the bill on a very very positive basis in order to try to get a bill that was fair to all those who were likely to be affected and to ensure that when it was put in place there would not be too much of a need to go back second-guessing.I conclude by saying that at some point New Zealand might want to consider whether some of our very good local events like the Rugby Sevens should come under these provisions or provisions like them. Perhaps there could be a lesser test. Perhaps it is something that might be devolved to local authorities. Good evidence was put in front of us that there is some need for this particularly around the issue of scalping.RON MARK (NZ First): I will take just a short call. I want to make it very very clear that New Zealand First supports this legislation. We have spoken on this bill and although we have noted with interest the rather curious situation we see unfolding in front of us here as we reflect on the past and on the history of this nation we support the legislation. We told the Minister Trevor Mallard that quite some time ago. He has our absolute and whole support.We believe that the arguments Mr Brownlee has just concluded making are all valid but it would be remiss of this Committee not to pass comment on some rather curious developments would it not? To not pass those comments would leave the people of the mind that we had totally ignored history and ignored reality and that we were not considering in full what we were doing—and we have. I ask the Committee to consider this. Let us take Mr Brownlee’s last comment about what we may consider as major events in the future. His suggestion that the Rugby Sevens could be classed a major event is I think a very valid sort of observation to make at this point in time. It might well be that should there be a grand soccer match—just one game—there could be an argument that that could be a major event. Let us suppose for example that New Zealand was to meet England in a match to be played here in Wellington at the Westpac Stadium. There could be an argument given the huge popularity and the huge viewing audience internationally that that one game could be considered a major event.Imagine an event such as the Rugby Sevens or even the Rugby World Cup where Zimbabwe entered a team. What might be the reaction amongst normal New Zealanders? It might well be that a bunch of former Zimbabwean citizens now New Zealand citizens might decide to protest and picket that match because of their hatred and loathing of the dictatorship led by Robert Mugabe. What this legislation brings to mind is that any pitch invasion of a Zimbabwe versus New Zealand game would be breaking the law.Hon Trevor Mallard: As it is now.RON MARK: That is what we are saying. Right now that is illegal and we are seeking to close the loophole so it is absolutely possible to control that contain that and deal with it.Some of us would say it is a good thing that those people should not be allowed to disrupt a match or to impinge on people’s rights to freely associate with whom they wish and that their rights to protest should be constrained contained and protected as much as other people’s rights to associate with other rugby teams and other rugby playing nations. In fact Judge Thurgood Marshall the pre-eminent black Americanwho became the most senior member of the judiciary in the United States and who was widely regarded as a leader and a campaigner for civil liberties and for the rights of black Americans argued that when he dealt with the case involving those who protested against the Springbok American tour in 1981 and wanted it stopped. He made sure that the rights of those who protested were looked after. They could protest near the venue but not in the venue. He made sure that the rights of those players to play whomever they wanted to play were not interrupted. No one was permitted to invade those pitches as he had seen from the television footage had happened here in New Zealand in the months prior to the Springboks tour of the United States. Of course the Eagles got hammered but the games were played.I think that is the fundamental thing we need to recognise. Everybody has their rights—the right to associate and the right to express a contrary opinion. It is a question of striking that balance through the middle to ensure that nobody’s rights are impinged on and that everybody is given the opportunity to do what they should be entitled to do but without adversely impacting on others. I think that is what New Zealand First likes about this legislation. We seek to protect the interests of both. The people who wish to protest can do so but not on the pitch not at the expense of people who have bought tickets and paid good money to go to see those games and not at the expense of the investors who are bringing the event to this nation. That is why we support the legislation.But we notice the delicious irony when one looks at these things as they have been presented right now. Mr Mallard the Minister I know on many occasions has told the nation that he was one of the protesters during 1981. I actually recall that time as being quite a difficult time because our family was split right through the middle between those of us who just wanted to watch the two best rugby playing nations in the world compete and those who wanted to stand and champion the cause against apartheid. Obviously some of us still feel to this day that the fact that matches were not played was a direct impingement on our right to see those matches played.Dr RICHARD WORTH (National): “Innovative” and “insightful” would be epithets that can appropriately be applied to the workings of the Commerce Committee under the skilled chairmanship of Mr Brownlee. I can illustrate that in a number of ways but let me just content myself with one illustration—it reflects badly on the Minister and that is why I have chosen it—and that is the use in this Major Events Management Bill of examples. I completely agree with what an earlier speaker has said: that it is wholly consistent with the new drafting style that this Parliament has adopted to use examples and diagrams. We have done it in tax legislation in the past. Here we see through this legislation examples of examples. It should be noted in dealing with this particular part that the status of examples is made clear in clause 4A. An example it is said is only illustrative of the provisions to which it relates. It does not limit those provisions. If there is an example and an inconsistent provision then it is the provision that prevails.The background to this legislation is reasonably simple. It is a matter of history that in association with New Zealand’s bids to host the 2011 Rugby World Cup and co-host the 2015 Cricket World Cup the Government has given assurances to the International Rugby Board and the International Cricket Council that certain protections will be put in place for those fixtures. Those protections include ambush marketing protections. More events of course are contemplated. New Zealand is to host the World Rowing Championships in 2010 and my understanding is that we will host the Under-17 Women’s Football World Cup in 2008.In Part 1 of the bill—which as others have commented contains preliminary provisions including purpose provisions—there are a raft of definitions in clause 4“Interpretation”. I just pick up on a couple of those definitions for the purpose of developing an argument around ambush marketing. There are definitions of “clean period” “clean transport route” and “clean zone”. A very substantial part of this legislation is around the concept of ambush marketing. It is quite a sophisticated phrase and quite a sophisticated concept that describes the actions of companies or advertisers who seek to associate themselves with major events or to draw a connection in the public’s mind between themselves and major events without the authorisation of the event organisers.There are a number of things that ambush marketers do. The types of things would range from arranging aerial advertising over a major event venue immediately before the event or during the event so as to suggest sponsorship or association to the placement of billboards displaying the ambush marketer’s name near the venue—or if there are several venues near the venues—or on key routes to the venue. Within the realm of ambush marketing there are other issues such as the unauthorised sale of goods or services at the event. There are issues associated with the registration and the exploitation of domain names. The aspect of scalping is also within ambush marketing. Scalping is the unauthorised on-sale of tickets—and I know we will look at it in some detail when we get to Part 2—but it is also all about the circumstance where tickets are packaged with travel and hospitality offerings. The subtlety of ambush marketing extends to the circumstance of giving away free merchandise such as caps or T-shirts bearing the advertiser’s logo with the aim that large groups of spectators will wear that gear and the television cameras will pan on to them or perhaps still photographs will be taken that hint at that association.There are—and this is picked up in the legislation and starts in this particular part—two main varieties of ambush marketing currently recognised in the context of major international events. I will return to that issue later.KEITH LOCKE (Green): I think Ron Mark was right to bring in the honourable tradition of political protest in New Zealand sometimes involving a presence on sports fields. He mentioned the possible situation of people protesting the situation in Zimbabwe and we had the 1981 anti - Springbok tour demonstrations. Generally the people taking part in this civil disobedience as it were do not expect not to be prosecuted; no one is saying that. But one does not need to put in a whole big penalty on it. Up to date it has fallen under the offence of disorderly behaviour. Sure disorderly behaviour has a maximum penalty of 3 months in jail but there has never been any thought by judges of imposing a prison sentence on streakers or people involved in this activity.What happened to the political protestors was that they actually got a lower penalty in the courts in 1981 or more recently in Britain they have completely got off for their civil disobedience around the Trident programme. The judges said: “Well because you were well motivated and you weren’t doing it for selfish reasons we’ll reduce the penalty.” That is effectively what has happened. But this bill is going totally in the opposite direction and saying that this is such a bad offence that we have to get beyond just a small fine under the heading of disorderly behaviour and put in a whole new offence with a maximum penalty of 3 months in prison and get all hot and bothered about it. That is completely the wrong way to go.I reject what Gerry Brownlee says that somehow I am encouraging pitch invasions. That is not the case at all. But let us face it—if someone does a little streak on a field that may not disrupt the game more than the average injury stoppage. Historically a lot of the time streakers have been a welcome extra element for many spectators. I am not encouraging streakers but I am just saying that we do not need to think that we theParliament have to bring in a whole new offence to get around a problem that really does not exist.Gerry Brownlee went on to say we were encouraging New Zealand business. In fact if members look at the sponsors of the Rugby World Cup in France this year I do not think they will see any French firms amongst the major sponsors—there may be sponsors but the major sponsors are not French. I do not expect that a single New Zealand firm will be a major sponsor in 2011 at the Rugby World Cup. The sponsors will be the same as at the present time—McDonald’s and Coca-Cola etc. who are the sponsors at the Rugby World Cup in France. New Zealand businesses will be out of the picture and this law will be used against them if they advertise even several kilometres from the venue.In terms of the definition of a major event I think under the definition here it could apply to a number of events in addition to the 2011 Rugby World Cup because one needs in terms of the definition as printed a large number of international participants or spectators therefore generating significant tourism opportunities.We could interpret that as a Bledisloe Cup match or possibly a Waratahs versus Auckland Sevens final or the International Sevens if it gets the tourist traffic that the Hong Kong Sevens gets. That could apply to those events. They attract significant sponsorship and media coverage. I think that would already apply to a number of the events I have mentioned and it would raise New Zealand’s international profile. It could apply to a number of events including the Rugby Sevens. Most of the action is actually in the stands at the Rugby Sevens. There is good action on the field too but it is quite conceivable that come early next year the odd dressed-up spectator will wander on to the field. That could happen in future similar tournaments including the Rugby World Cup. But I would ask why we really want to jump all over such people with a heavier penalty. Do we not have better things to do?SureThe Lord of the Ringsattracted a lot of international attention to New Zealand but the difference between that and the Rugby World Cup is not just the number of people who might watch the TV feed of the Rugby World Cup—and I agree with Gerry Brownlee that it may be greater than the number of people who watchedThe Lord of the Rings—but is also thatThe Lord of the Rings did not impinge on the human rights of New Zealanders. This bill does.The question was put that the amendment set out on Supplementary Order Paper 138 in the name of Keith Locke to clause 4 to omit and substitute words be agreed to.A party vote was called for on the questionThat the amendment be agreed to.Ayes10Green Party 6; Māori Party 4.Noes109New Zealand Labour 49; New Zealand National 48; New Zealand First 7; United Future 2; Progressive 1; Independents: Copeland Field.Amendment not agreed to.The question was put that the amendment set out on Supplementary Order Paper 138 in the name of Keith Locke to clause 4 to omit a definition be agreed to.A party vote was called for on the questionThat the amendment be agreed to.Ayes6Green Party 6.Noes112New Zealand Labour 49; New Zealand National 48; New Zealand First 7; Māori Party 3; United Future 2; Progressive 1; Independents: Copeland Field.Amendment not agreed to.A party vote was called for on the questionThat Part 1 be agreed to.Ayes109New Zealand Labour 49; New Zealand National 48; New Zealand First 7; United Future 2; Progressive 1; Independents: Copeland Field.Noes9Green Party 6; Māori Party 3.Part 1 agreed to.Part 2 Declaration of major event and the protections for major eventsALLAN PEACHEY (National—Tamaki): I appreciate having the opportunity to speak also to Part 2 and I just note with a little irony that most of what the representatives of the Green Party and New Zealand First have been talking about is probably more relevant to Part 2 than it was to Part 1.I will take the opportunity to share with Mr Mark his love of American history. Twice in this debate he has mentioned Justice Thurgood Marshall. I think that if he checks his history he will find that Justice Marshall although appointed to the Supreme Court by Lyndon Johnson in I think 1965 never actually reached the top echelons of the Supreme Court. He was simply an associate and never a Chief Justice. It pays to get one’s history right.Previous speakers have traversed a range of views and interpretations of this legislation and I wonder what those very fine people at the New Zealand Rugby Union and their business interests must be thinking. Here we are with a fair degree of unanimity in the Committee debating very very important legislation that will lay out the environment for New Zealand to host the Rugby World Cup 2011 very very well. It is important we do it well because this sort of activity will be more and more important to our future not only in hosting the event itself and getting that right but also in enjoying the downstream advantages that come from that with New Zealand being seen as a destination for doing business tourism and that sort of thing. Yet here members are finding themselves almost degenerating into irrelevancies about the way that people might conduct themselves.I want to make one thing very clear to the Committee: New Zealanders are sick and tired of having their enjoyment of major events spoilt by one or two self-important indulgent people who think that making an exhibition of themselves is far more important than the game that is under way.
